name: Internal Port Scan
title: Port scan detected from {src_ip2} against {server}
description: Firewall logged 30+ denied connections from internal host {src_ip2} against sequential ports on {server} in under a minute — consistent with network service discovery.
objective: Identify the source of the internal port scan against {server} and determine whether it is authorized scanning (vulnerability management) or attacker reconnaissance.
checklist: Run the detection query and characterize the scan (ports, speed, pattern)
Identify which host owns {src_ip2} and who was logged into it
Check whether the source is a known vulnerability scanner or IT tool
Look for successful connections that followed the scan (did anything answer?)
Check for scans against other targets from the same source
hints: Sequential destination ports at machine speed = scanning. Search index=network src_ip={src_ip2} to see the full pattern.
Real environments have authorized scanners (Nessus, Qualys) on known IPs. This environment's scanner subnet is 10.20.9.x — is {src_ip2} in it?

Recon from a workstation IP is a red flag: workstations don't scan servers. But with no follow-on exploitation activity, this may only warrant escalation to check the workstation, not a full incident.
expectedEvidence: Burst of denied connections across sequential ports from {src_ip2}
Source is a user workstation, not an authorized scanner
Presence or absence of follow-on connection attempts
Owner/logged-in user of the source host
explanation: This is unauthorized network discovery from a user workstation — either a curious user running a scanning tool or, more concerning, an attacker's foothold mapping the network. Port scans themselves cause no damage, which is why the severity is low, but they are a leading indicator: recon precedes lateral movement. The disposition logic: it's not a false positive (the scan really happened, from an unauthorized source), but a lone scan without exploitation doesn't confirm compromise either — it needs escalation to examine the source workstation. In a real SOC you would also check the scan source against your VM tool's IP allowlist before spending any more time.
reportTemplate: 1. Contact the owner of the workstation at {src_ip2}. 2. Review process history on that host around scan time. 3. If no user explanation, treat the workstation as potentially compromised. 4. Verify scanner allowlists are current to reduce future noise.

events.message: action=denied proto=tcp src={src_ip2} dst={src_ip} dport={port} flags=SYN
action=allowed proto=tcp src={src_ip2} dst={src_ip} dport=445 flags=SYN
